item.title: Govt to introduce new bills for enforced disappearances and other lapsed ordinances
item.description:
The government has announced that, although the ordinances concerning the appointment of Supreme Court judges and the establishment of a separate secretariat have been repealed, it will introduce new bills in Parliament following consultations with relevant stakeholders.

At the same time, the government stated that it would conduct further scrutiny and subsequently introduce new bills concerning 16 ordinancesŌĆöincluding those on the prevention of enforced disappearances, the Police Commission, and amendments to the Anti-Corruption CommissionŌĆöthat were not approved or rejected by Parliament within the stipulated timeframe.

However, no specific timeline has been provided regarding when these bills will be tabled.

At a press conference held yesterday, Sunday, Home Minister Salahuddin Ahmed, Law Minister Asaduzzaman, and Chief Whip of the Jatiya Sangsad Nurul Islam outlined the governmentŌĆÖs position. The briefing took place in the conference room of the Ministry of Home Affairs.

During the tenure of the interim government, a total of 133 ordinances were promulgated. In accordance with parliamentary procedure, these were presented at the first sitting of the first session of the 13th Parliament on 12 March.

The deadline for their approval or rejection was 10 April. Within this period, 16 ordinances were not introduced in the form of bills.

As a result, these ordinances have lapsed. Additionally, seven ordinances were annulled through repeal bills, while the remaining 110 ordinances were approved by Parliament, some in amended form.

A special parliamentary committee, comprising members from both the ruling party and the opposition, was formed to review the ordinances.

However, not all of the committeeŌĆÖs recommendations were followed in full during the approval process.

For instance, the committee had recommended that the "July mass uprising memorial museum ordinance" be approved without alteration.

Nevertheless, when amendments were introduced to the related bill on Friday, it led to prolonged debate between the ruling party and the opposition in Parliament.

The opposition staged a walkout that day, accusing the government of ŌĆ£political compromise and breach of trustŌĆØ for failing to present several significant ordinancesŌĆöincluding those on enforced disappearances and the Police CommissionŌĆöin bill form.

Addressing the issue at the press conference, Salahuddin Ahmed stated that, under parliamentary norms, the opposition has the right to walk out.

However, he added that the oppositionŌĆÖs actions were based on inaccurate information in several instances, saying that they had ŌĆ£placed blame on the government on various matters.ŌĆØ

He further remarked, ŌĆ£The July memorial museum ordinance had a recommendation for approval in its original form. The bill was introduced accordingly. If a private member proposes an amendment and it appears reasonable to Parliament or the government, it may be adopted through a vote.ŌĆØ

He added that the opposition had still been given the opportunity to speak on the matter, and that the law minister had indicated in Parliament that the bill could be brought back later in a more refined form.

It is pertinent to note that any member of parliament may propose amendments to a bill. However, the acceptance of such amendments depends largely on the ruling party.

Initially, the relevant minister indicates whether they support the amendment, after which it is put to a vote. Given the ruling partyŌĆÖs majority, amendments are typically adopted if they choose to support them, often following prior party direction.

At the same press conference, Salahuddin Ahmed noted that the opposition had also raised concerns regarding certain ordinances.

He stated that the government intends to engage with stakeholders to further develop and refine key ordinancesŌĆöincluding those on the prevention of enforced disappearances, the Human Rights Commission, the Supreme Court Secretariat, and the appointment of Supreme Court judgesŌĆöbefore reintroducing them in the next parliamentary session.

He emphasised that inclusive consultation would strengthen these laws for the benefit of the nation.

Law Minister Asaduzzaman added that, of the 133 ordinances issued during the interim governmentŌĆÖs tenure, the 16 not presented in Parliament require further examination, which is why they have not yet been tabled.

Rejecting allegations from certain quarters that the government has abandoned these ordinances, he stated that the principles of transparency and accountability are clearly articulated in the preambles of the proposed laws. He urged all concerned not to spread misinformation on the matter.

Judicial independence and the human rights commission

The special parliamentary committee recommended the repeal of four ordinances: the National Parliament Secretariat (Interim Special Provisions) Ordinance, the Supreme Court Judges Appointment Ordinance, the Supreme Court Secretariat Ordinance 2025, and the Supreme Court Secretariat (Amendment) Ordinance 2026. Parliament subsequently annulled these through the passage of repeal bills.

Conversely, regarding three ordinances related to the National Human Rights Commission, the committee recommended deferring their introduction as bills and instead conducting further scrutiny before presenting new legislation at a later stage.

However, Parliament repealed these ordinances and passed a bill reinstating the National Human Rights Commission Act of 2009.

Addressing the matter, Law Minister Asaduzzaman stated that the repeal bills concerning the National Human Rights Commission Ordinance, the Supreme Court Judges Appointment Ordinance, and the Supreme Court Secretariat Ordinance explicitly mention the need for further consultation and detailed review.

In response to a question from journalists regarding the distinction between the current situation and that of 2007ŌĆō08ŌĆöwhen a previous interim government issued several ordinances related to the judiciary and public matters that were later not approved by ParliamentŌĆöAsaduzzaman referred to the statement of objects and reasons attached to the bills.

He said, ŌĆ£The point of difference is that the previous government did not introduce bills stating that further scrutiny was necessary. We have introduced bills and committed that we will undertake further examination and hold discussions with stakeholders before bringing them back.ŌĆØ

It is pertinent to note that every bill presented in Parliament includes a statement outlining its objectives and reasons, although this statement does not form part of the law itself.

Responding to a question on the judiciary, Home Minister Salahuddin Ahmed cited the Constitution and stated, ŌĆ£Judges are independent in conducting judicial proceedings. Has it been said that they are independent in any other matters? We want the independence of the judiciary. But if, in the context of the issues you are raising, the judiciary becomes super independent or sovereign, will that serve the interests of the state?ŌĆØ

He further remarked that there must be ŌĆ£harmonious co-operationŌĆØ among the organs of the state, ensuring that none oversteps its boundaries. For this reason, he emphasised the need to enact ŌĆ£very balancedŌĆØ laws concerning the Supreme Court Secretariat and the appointment of judges.

Referendum ŌĆśFactum ValetŌĆÖ

As Parliament neither approved nor rejected the Referendum Ordinance within the stipulated time, it has lapsed. The opposition has threatened to launch a movement over the issue.

When asked for his reaction, Salahuddin Ahmed stated that it is a matter of ŌĆ£factum valet,ŌĆØ meaning something validated by the fact of its occurrence.

He explained that the ordinance had been promulgated, the referendum had taken place, and it carries legal validity.

Salahuddin Ahmed further stated that no future referendums will take place under this law. If a referendum is required in the future, it must be conducted in accordance with the Constitution or under separate legislation.

He added that once the Fourth Schedule of the Constitution validates most of the actions of the previous interim governmentŌĆöbarring one or two exceptionsŌĆöthose actions will become legally valid.

However, he also emphasised that there is no scope for creating confusion regarding the referendum.

A writ petition has already been filed before the court challenging the validity of the referendum that has taken place, and the matter now depends on the courtŌĆÖs ruling.

Enforced disappearance ordinance

The enforced disappearance ordinance promulgated by the interim Government has already lapsed. The home minister and the law minister explained the reasons for not presenting it for approval.

Referring to his own experience as a victim of enforced disappearance, Home Minister Salahuddin Ahmed stated that, if the ordinance were passed in its current form, the victims themselves would face injustice.

He emphasised that the government intends to establish a commission on enforced disappearances in a manner that ensures both its effectiveness and the delivery of justice to victims.

He further remarked that, during the interim governmentŌĆÖs tenure, certain individuals acted hastily in introducing the Human Rights Commission and Enforced Disappearance ordinances in an attempt to ŌĆ£provideŌĆØ for themselves (secure positions).

However, he added that he did not wish to assign blame. He noted that a government with a tenure of only 18 months had sought to implement these measures, and therefore some shortcomings were inevitable.

He stated that a political government is now in place and has sufficient time to proceed carefully.

Through consultation, the government aims to ensure coherence among the laws relating to the Enforced Disappearance Commission, the Human Rights Commission, and the International Crimes Tribunal (ICT). He added that this process requires time.

Salahuddin Ahmed said, ŌĆ£We have spoken with these matters in mind. However, members of the opposition have politicised these issues, which is something we do not wish to do.ŌĆØ

On this matter, Law Minister Md Asaduzzaman stated that the law contains certain ambiguities regarding the definition of enforced disappearance and the judicial process, which could create legal complications in the future.

He also noted concerns regarding several provisions of the Human Rights Commission law, particularly the lack of clear guidance in relation to investigation, fines, and compensation. He indicated that a consultative meeting on the Human Rights Commission law may be held after mid-May.

Police commission

The special parliamentary committee had recommended passing the Police Commission Ordinance in an amended form; however, this did not take place, and the ordinance has now lapsed.

In response to journalistsŌĆÖ questions as to why the ordinance was not introduced in Parliament in line with the recommendation, Salahuddin Ahmed stated that the ordinance had neither created any posts nor involved any financial expenditure.

Therefore, there was no necessity to retain it immediately. He added that there is no obligation to present it during the current session and that it will be introduced in Parliament later in a revised form.

16 lapsed ordinances

Earlier, reports on Saturday indicated that 13 ordinances had not been introduced in the form of bills. However, the Ministry of Law clarified yesterday, Sunday that the total number is 16.

These include: the Referendum Ordinance; two ordinances relating to the prevention and remedy of enforced disappearance; the Anti-Corruption Commission (Amendment) Ordinance; the Police Commission Ordinance; the Civil Aviation (Amendment) Ordinance; the Bangladesh Travel Agency (Registration and Regulation) (Amendment) Ordinance; the Microfinance Bank Ordinance; the Private Educational Institutions Teachers and Employees Retirement Benefits (Amendment) Ordinance; two ordinances concerning revenue policy and revenue management; the Value Added Tax and Supplementary Duty (Second Amendment) Ordinance; the Customs (Amendment) Ordinance; the Income Tax (Amendment) Ordinance; the Private Educational Institutions Teachers and Employees Welfare Trust (Amendment) Ordinance; and the Right to Information (Amendment) Ordinance.

item.title: US, Indonesia discussing agreement allowing US military overflight in Indonesian airspace, Indonesian ministry says
item.description:
JAKARTA, April 13 (Reuters) - Indonesia and ŌĆīthe United States are discussing ŌĆŗan ŌĆŗagreement to give U.S. ŌüĀmilitary aircraft ŌĆŗaccess to ŌĆŗIndonesian airspace, Indonesia's defence ministry said ŌĆŗon Monday, ŌĆŗadding that no deal ŌĆīhas ŌüĀyet been reached.
U.S. Defence Secretary Pete ŌĆŗHegseth ŌĆŗis ŌüĀset to meet ŌĆŗwith his ŌĆŗIndonesian ŌüĀcounterpart Sjafrie Sjamsoeddin later ŌüĀon ŌĆŗMonday.

item.title: Two killed in Ukraine despite Easter ceasefire, governor says
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Two people were killed and one injured by Russian troops in Ukraine's frontline eastern Donetsk region ŌĆŗbefore the expiry of a ceasefire to mark Orthodox ŌĆīEaster , a local official said on Monday,
One person was killed in the city of Kramatorsk and one in Druzhkivka ŌĆŗon Sunday, regional governor Vadym Filashkin said on ŌĆŗthe Telegram messaging app, without giving more details.
Both ŌüĀRussia and Ukraine have accused each other of breaching ŌĆŗthe 32-hour ceasefire from Saturday afternoon to midnight (2100 GMT) ŌĆŗon Sunday.
Ukraine's Coordination Headquarters for the Treatment of Prisoners of War, citing a spokesperson of the 14th Army Corps, said on ŌĆŗMonday that during "the so-called 'Easter ceasefire'" Russian servicemen also ŌĆŗshot dead four Ukrainian prisoners of war in the Kharkiv region.
It ŌĆīdid ŌüĀnot give more details, adding that Russia was preparing a campaign to deny it.
There was no immediate comment from Russia and Reuters could not verify the ŌĆŗinformation independently.
The General ŌĆŗStaff of ŌüĀUkraine's military said on Monday it had compiled a total of 10,721 Russian ŌĆŗviolations over the course of the truce.
It ŌĆŗsaid the ŌüĀviolations included 1,567 artillery shelling incidents, 119 assault operations and 9,035 strikes by attack drones. It said no ŌüĀair ŌĆŗstrikes had been recorded.
The Russian ŌĆŗdefence ministry has said it recorded 1,971 ceasefire violations by Ukraine overnight ŌĆŗinto Sunday.

item.title: Kuwait raises May crude prices for Asia, pricing document shows
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Kuwait has raised ŌĆŗthe official selling ŌĆīprice for Kuwait Export ŌĆŗCrude to ŌĆŗAsia in May ŌüĀto $17 a ŌĆŗbarrel above ŌĆŗthe average of Oman/Dubai quotes, a ŌĆŗprice document ŌĆŗreviewed by Reuters ŌĆīshowed ŌüĀon Monday.
The producer increased the May Kuwait ŌĆŗSuper ŌĆŗLight ŌüĀcrude OSP to $17 ŌĆŗa barrel ŌĆŗabove ŌüĀOman/Dubai quotes.

item.title: PolandŌĆÖs premier hails Hungary vote as blow to authoritarian rule
item.description:
WARSAW, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungary's election shows that Europe is not on an inevitable path toward authoritarian rule, ŌĆŗPolish Prime Minister Donald Tusk said on Monday, ŌĆīafter the center-right Tisza party ended Viktor Orban's 16ŌĆæyear grip on power.
"Everyone feared there was a trend toward authoritarian, corrupt ŌĆŗregimes," Tusk said while on an official ŌĆŗvisit to South Korea , according to Polish state-owned ŌüĀnews agency PAP.
"That's not the case. First Warsaw, ŌĆŗthen Bucharest, Chisinau, now Budapest."
In 2025, centrist Nicusor Dan won ŌĆŗa presidential election in Romania and Moldova's pro-European ruling party won a resounding victory over its Russia-aligned rival.
Tusk had repeatedly criticized ŌĆŗOrban's government for its close ties with Moscow.
"I'm ŌĆŗglad that this part of Europe is showing that we ŌĆīare ŌüĀnot doomed to corrupt and authoritarian governments, because that's what Victor Orban's government unfortunately became after many years in power."
Neighbors Poland and Hungary are linked ŌĆŗby their long, ŌĆŗshared history, ŌüĀclose trade ties and cooperation within the European Union and NATO.
Tusk said he ŌĆŗhad spoken with Tisza leader Peter ŌĆŗMagyar ŌüĀto congratulate him on his victory.
"We briefly discussed his visit to Warsaw," Tusk said.
"As you know, he long ŌüĀago ŌĆŗchose Warsaw as his first ŌĆŗvisit for quite obvious reasons. I think our relationship will be ŌĆŗabsolutely exceptional."

item.title: Philippines warns of 'sabotage' after cyanide seizure in disputed South China Sea atoll
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - The Philippines discovered cyanide on Chinese boats operating around a ŌĆīdisputed atoll in the South China Sea, security officials from the country said on Monday.
Authorities said laboratory tests confirmed the presence of the highly toxic substance in bottles seized by the Philippine navy in operations at Second ŌĆŗThomas Shoal last year.
Officials warned the cyanide could have had serious consequences for marine life ŌĆŗand weakened the reef supporting a warship that Manila grounded on the ŌüĀatoll to reinforce its maritime claim.
"We wish to underscore that the use of cyanide in ŌĆŗAyungin Shoal is a form of sabotage that seeks to kill local fish populations, depriving ŌĆŗNavy personnel of a vital food source," Cornelio Valencia, National Security Council spokesperson, told a press conference using the Philippines' name for the atoll. Valencia added that cyanide could damage the reef and "ultimately compromise" the warship's stability.
The ŌĆŗChinese Embassy in Manila did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Philippines has ŌĆŗaccused China of disrupting resupply missions to troops on the vessel, including a June 17, 2024, incident that ŌĆīturned ŌüĀviolent and resulted in a Filipino sailor losing a finger. China has denied allegations of aggressive conduct during such encounters and accused the Philippines of trespassing in its waters.
The June confrontation later led to a provisional understanding for resupply missions to the grounded ship.
China and the ŌĆŗPhilippines held high-level talks ŌĆŗlast month over the ŌüĀSouth China Sea, exploring preliminary steps towards oil and gas cooperation , and confidence-building measures at sea, including communication between their coast guards.
The Philippine foreign ministry ŌĆŗsaid in a statement on Monday the scope of the coast ŌĆŗguard cooperation ŌüĀwould be limited, and "does not contemplate cooperation in sensitive operational areas," adding that there had been no discussions on joint patrols.
China claims nearly all the South China Sea, including areas claimed by ŌüĀBrunei, Malaysia, ŌĆŗthe Philippines and Vietnam.
A 2016 ruling by an international ŌĆŗarbitral tribunal found Beijing's sweeping claims had no basis under international law, a decision China rejects. More than $3 trillion ŌĆŗin annual ship-borne commerce travels through the key waterway.

item.title: Exclusive: Chilean copper miner Codelco, contractors fined after deadly mine collapse
item.description:
SANTIAGO, April 13 (Reuters) - Chilean state copper miner Codelco was fined by labor authorities after last year's deadly collapse at its El Teniente mine, while three contractors whose workers were killed or injured were sanctioned more heavily, according to inspection records obtained by Reuters through public-records requests.
The previously unreported sanctions were imposed in the months ŌĆŗfollowing the July 31 underground seismic event that triggered a rock burst at El Teniente , the world's largest underground copper mine, killing six contract workers and injuring others.
Reuters obtained the files from ŌĆīChile's labor ministry through open-records requests. In Chile, such fines are notified directly to employers and can be challenged or reduced administratively, but are not typically disclosed in public announcements.
At the time of the accident, then-Labor Minister Giorgio Boccardo said that his office and the mining regulator, Sernageomin, would investigate the causes of the incident and whether any labor-safety rules had been breached.
The quake, measured at about magnitude 4.3, led to a halt in all underground operations at the sprawling mine complex amid rescue efforts and safety inspections.
The collapse ŌĆŗcarried a significant production cost for Codelco. The company has said the shutdown of underground operations at El Teniente and a slow restart cut copper output by tens of thousands of metric tons, disrupting shipments at ŌĆŗa time of tight global supply.
The disaster also underscored the geotechnical risks facing Chile's aging underground mines .
The records show the three contractors were fined the ŌüĀequivalent of about $87,000 combined, versus roughly $20,000 for Codelco, a gap that reflects Chile's split-liability framework for subcontracted work.
While the principal company - Codelco - can be penalized for overarching safety failures, contractors remain directly responsible as employers for accident reporting, ŌĆŗrisk assessment, worker assignment and other compliance duties, under Chile's labor code.
In one sanction, labor inspectors said Codelco lacked a complete, written procedure showing how seismic warnings were used to decide whether work should stop or be restricted.
After the ŌĆŗaccident, regulators also found that Codelco violated labor rules when workers were found entering, or preparing to enter, underground areas while the mine-wide suspension remained in force, according to a separate sanction record.
Under Chilean labor rules, some serious or fatal-accident violations can be fined at up to 150 UTM, an inflation-linked Chilean tax unit, or about $11,000 at current values, per infraction. The regulator levied a 340 UTM penalty, roughly $26,000 in today's terms, on a company after a fatal construction-site collapse in a 2007 case.
Labor advocates and worker-safety specialists ŌĆŗhave questioned whether such small penalties carry enough deterrent force for major employers.
"It is essential to raise the size of fines in order to effectively deter companies from violating mining safety regulations," a Chilean House of Representatives investigative ŌĆŗcommission report said in 2011 after a mining disaster.
Proposals since then to hike up fines for serious or fatal workplace accidents have not gotten off the ground.
Since the collapse, Codelco has said it tightened safety procedures for restarting work at ŌĆīEl Teniente, ŌüĀadding safety briefings at the start of shifts, improving communications underground, stepping up checks on workers' locations and reviewing protective equipment.
It later said an independent panel led by a former Anglo American CEO was examining what caused the accident and whether broader management or workplace problems played a role.
In a statement to Reuters, Codelco said that its seismic alert response system was active the day of the accident, and that it had appealed the fine by the labor ministry.
It added that there was an "ongoing legal proceeding related to the oversight of worker entry during the work stoppage" and that it was awaiting a decision from authorities.
Codelco said in August that El Teniente mine manager Andres Music would leave ŌĆŗhis role, and in February announced the departure of three ŌĆŗsenior executives after an internal audit found inconsistencies and ŌüĀconcealment following a separate rock burst at the mine several years earlier.
Among the three contracting firms fined, Strabag (STRV.VI) , opens new tab subsidiary Zublin was fined for failing to report a worker's death within 24 hours. Inspectors found that although the company knew about the fatality within two hours, it didn't notify labor authorities until the following evening.
Immediate reporting ŌĆŗis critical to implement emergency safety measures to protect remaining workers, the report said.
The Austrian company did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
A unit of ŌĆŗChilean construction firm SalfaCorp (SALFACORP.SN) , opens new tab was also ŌüĀsanctioned after one of its workers died in the Andesita sector of the mine. Inspectors said the company failed to immediately report the fatal accident to authorities, in addition to other offenses.
The records also said Salfa's risk assessment did not adequately account for seismic hazards and that the company failed to do enough to protect workers' lives.
SalfaCorp did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Chile's labor regulator also fined Constructora Gardilcic, the unlisted contractor whose workers were killed and injured in ŌüĀthe Recursos Norte ŌĆŗarea of the mine.
Inspectors said the company reported the accident late, delayed filing injury reports and showed weak safety planning.
Authorities also concluded Gardilcic ŌĆŗdid not adequately account for the risk of violent rock bursts outside designated danger zones and put some workers in jobs they were not cleared to do.
Gardilcic did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Codelco has said the areas hardest hit by the accident ŌĆŗwill remain under tight restrictions while criminal , regulatory and technical investigations continue.
The company has pledged a gradual, regulator-approved restart, leaving uncertainty over when the entire mine can resume normal operations.

item.title: UK will not back blockade of Strait of Hormuz, PM Starmer says
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said on ŌĆŗMonday that whatever the pressure, Britain would not be dragged into ŌĆīthe Iran war nor be involved in a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.
"We're not supporting the blockade," he told BBC Radio 5 Live, adding that it was ŌĆŗvital to get the Strait reopened.
"It is, in my view, vital ŌĆŗthat we get the Strait open and fully open, and ŌüĀthat's where we've put all of our efforts in the last ŌĆŗfew and we'll continue to do so," Starmer said.
Britain had minesweepers in the ŌĆŗregion, he said, and while he could not discuss operational matters, the military capability was "focused from our point of view on getting the Strait full open".
The U.S. military said it ŌĆŗwould begin a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and exiting ŌĆŗIranian ports and coastal areas on Monday, after weekend talks failed to reach a deal ŌĆīto ŌüĀend the war with Iran.
The U.S. Central Command said that the U.S. blockade, starting at 10 a.m. ET on Monday (1400 GMT), would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal ŌĆŗareas, including all ŌĆŗIranian ports on ŌüĀthe Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman."
Vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports will ŌĆŗnot be impeded, the U.S. military said.
President Donald Trump ŌĆŗsaid on ŌüĀSunday U.S. forces would also intercept every vessel in international waters that had paid a toll to Iran.
"No one who pays an illegal toll will ŌüĀhave safe ŌĆŗpassage on the high seas," Trump wrote ŌĆŗon social media, adding: "Any Iranian who fires at us, or at peaceful vessels, will be ŌĆŗBLOWN TO HELL!"

item.title: Iran's Hormuz gamble ushers in a tense new normal for Gulf energy
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - The Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz have shattered a status quo that prevailed among Middle East oil and gas producers for decades. Even if the current, shaky ceasefire holds, the uneasy ŌĆ£new normalŌĆØ has likely set the stage for yet another round of conflict.
Tehran has now demonstrated both its ability and willingness to seal off the critical waterway and strike vital ŌĆŗinfrastructure across the region, fundamentally altering the risk calculus for its neighbours and jeopardizing Gulf countriesŌĆÖ longŌĆæterm oil and gas strategies.
The sixŌĆæweek war laid bare deep tensions between the Islamic Republic and its ŌĆīkey regional neighbours Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Bahrain and Iraq - all close U.S. allies. They had long avoided direct confrontation with Tehran under a tacit understanding that war would devastate their shared economic interests. But that entente cordiale has now been blown apart.
At the heart of the crisis lies the Strait of Hormuz . Iran closed the waterway for the first time in history, trapping nearly a fifth of the worldŌĆÖs oil and gas supplies inside the Gulf, delivering a devastating shock to the region and the global economy. Even if shipping ultimately resumes, ŌĆŗthe unprecedented act marks a historic rupture.
Iran has signalled that it wants to retain leverage over the strait under any future peace deal, floating the idea of controlling traffic and charging vessels a transit fee. U.S. President Donald ŌĆŗTrump has urged Tehran to fully reopen the waterway, and on Sunday said the U.S. Navy would immediately start blockading the strait, raising the stakes after marathon talks with Iran failed to ŌüĀreach a deal to end the war .
Yet even a formal reopening would do little to erase the lesson the war has taught IranŌĆÖs neighbours. The threat of Iran closing the strait - now proven feasible with limited military effort - is a genie ŌĆŗthat cannot be put back into the bottle.
The war has sharply exposed how vulnerable the regionŌĆÖs energy infrastructure is.
Fighting between the U.S., Israel and Iran quickly spilled across borders as Iranian missiles and drones struck dozens of targets in neighbouring ŌĆŗcountries, including major oil and gas facilities across the Gulf.
Around 11 million barrels per day of oil production and QatarŌĆÖs entire LNG output were forced offline, while refineries, export terminals and other critical installations , opens new tab suffered extensive damage.
For Saudi Arabia, the worldŌĆÖs largest crude exporter and de facto leader of OPEC, the implications are especially troubling. Even the alternative export route it painstakingly developed in recent decades to bypass Hormuz proved vulnerable.
The kingdomŌĆÖs EastŌĆæWest Pipeline, designed to divert roughly 7 million barrels per day from the eastern oil heartland to the Red Sea port of Yanbu, ŌĆŗwas hit within hours of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire announcement.
Saudi Arabia, which exported around 8 million bpd of oil before the war, said the attacks had cut its oil production capacity by around 600,000 bpd and reduced throughput on the EastŌĆæWest Pipeline by about ŌĆŗ700,000 bpd.
At the same time, the UAEŌĆÖs oil export pipeline to the port of Fujairah , which sits outside the strait, was also struck repeatedly. And for Qatar and Kuwait, the strait remains the sole export route.
While this situation might look like a checkmate for Tehran, ŌĆīthe Gulf is ŌüĀmore apt to see it as an intolerable status quo that will need to be remedied.
This new reality strikes at the core of the GulfŌĆÖs economic model ŌĆō both now and in the future.
For starters, the region faces years of reconstruction and instability that will undermine national development plans, even if the elevated geopolitical risk premium keeps energy prices higher.
The long-term threat is even greater.
The war has jolted many nations - particularly in Asia - into a painful reassessment of energy import dependence. For the Gulf, that comes at the worst possible moment: producers were already facing the need to maximise exports in the face of potentially diminishing demand as major importers shift away from fossil fuels. The conflict will only accelerate it.
For Gulf producers who ŌĆŗexpect uninterrupted exports of oil, liquefied natural gas, refined ŌĆŗproducts, chemicals and fertilizer to fund their economies for ŌüĀdecades to come, uncertainty over Hormuz is simply unsustainable, not only geopolitically but economically.
Consequently, the key regional powers, Saudi Arabia and the UAE - both of which harbour international ambitions economically and geopolitically - are unlikely to accept any strategic reality in which Iran can constrain their goals over the long term. That, in turn, points to a heightened risk of future confrontation.
"The strait ŌĆŗmust be open - fully, unconditionally and without restriction. Energy security and global economic stability depend on it. The weaponization of this vital waterway, in any form, cannot stand," ŌĆŗSultan Al Jaber, the CEO of ŌüĀUAE state oil giant ADNOC, said last week .
The terms of whatever "permanent" peace deal the parties eventually agree to will matter less than the fact that this war has broken a longŌĆæstanding taboo: targeting vital economic infrastructure in the Gulf is no longer off limits.
That shift alone makes the regional balance of power more volatile, casting a long shadow over the Middle East for years to come.
The rally in global equities and the decline in oil prices following last week's ceasefire announcement suggest investors believe the Middle ŌüĀEast will soon ŌĆŗreturn to business as usual.
They are almost certainly wrong. The most likely outcome is not a restoration of the old order but a new ŌĆŗnormal - one in which Gulf powers seek to ensure Iran can never again wield the Hormuz trump card.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid ŌĆŗdaily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Iran, US 'sincere' about ceasefire: Turkey foreign minister
item.description:
PAKISTAN-WAR-IRAN-US-ISRAEL

Turkey's foreign minister said on Monday he believed both Iran and the United States remain "sincere" about reaching a ceasefire despite the failure of Pakistan-mediated talks on the weekend.

US President Donald Trump has blamed the Islamic republic's refusal to abandon its nuclear ambitions for the collapse of the talks.

"Both sides are sincere about the ceasefire," Hakan Fidan said in an interview with the official Anadolu news agency, adding that he has been in contact with the parties involved in the negotiations.

"I am thinking that if the nuclear issue comes down to an all-or-nothing situation especially regarding enrichment, we might face serious obstacles," Fidan said.

"Hopefully, we will try to overcome this with the support of some mediators," he added.

Turkey, while a fierce critic of Israel, joined diplomatic efforts with Egypt and Pakistan to reach a ceasefire in the conflict.

item.title: Israeli fire kills three in Gaza following new ceasefire talks
item.description:
CAIRO/GAZA, April 13 (Reuters) - An Israeli airstrike killed at ŌĆŗleast three Palestinians in the Gaza Strip on Monday, health officials said, as mediators met leaders from ŌĆīHamas in an effort to shore up a U.S.-brokered ceasefire deal.
Medics said the strike had hit a group of men outside a school in Deir al-Balah in the central Gaza Strip. There was no immediate comment by the Israeli military.
At Al-Aqsa Hospital in Deir al-Balah, the bodies ŌĆŗof those killed lay on the ground in white shrouds outside the morgue as relatives and friends arrived ŌĆŗto bid them farewell. Some kissed the victims' foreheads before holding special prayers.
ŌĆ£This isnŌĆÖt a truce; ŌüĀitŌĆÖs a trap for our young men. Every day there are martyrs, every single day. How long can this continue?ŌĆØ ŌĆŗsaid Umm Hussam Abu El-Rous, a female relative of one of the victims.
ŌĆ£Isn't it unjust that a three-year-old child is afraid ŌĆŗof seeing his (dead) father? He says, 'My father went to bring me something from the shop,'" she added.
The ceasefire that began last October halted two years of full-blown war but left Israeli troops in control of a depopulated zone demarcated by yellow-painted blocks that comprises well over half of ŌĆŗGaza, with Hamas in power in a narrow coastal strip and Israeli airstrikes continuing .
More than 750 Palestinians have been killed since ŌĆŗthe deal took effect, while militants have killed four Israeli soldiers. Israel and Hamas have traded blame for ceasefire violations.
Palestinians say Israeli forces ŌĆīhave ŌüĀbeen moving some of the yellow concrete markers westward. Israel denies this.
The violence comes as leaders from Hamas and other Palestinian factions have been meeting since Saturday in Cairo with mediators from Egypt, Turkey, and Qatar to discuss implementing the second phase of the Gaza deal.
Under a plan put forward by U.S. President Donald Trump's Board of Peace , Hamas would be ŌĆŗrequired to lay down its arms ŌĆŗin stages over eight months ŌüĀafter a U.S.-backed committee of Palestinian technocrats takes control in Gaza.
However, Hamas' disarmament has been a major obstacle to progress on Trump's ceasefire deal and plan for Gaza , which have also ŌĆŗbeen put under strain by the war in Iran.
Two officials close to the latest talks ŌĆŗsaid Hamas told ŌüĀmediators that discussions on disarmament could only move forward after Israel fully implements the first phase of Trump's October deal, which includes a complete ceasefire in Gaza.
Israeli military officials have said they are preparing for a swift return to full-scale war if Hamas does ŌüĀnot ŌĆŗlay down its weapons.
The Gaza war began on October 7, 2023, with a ŌĆŗHamas-led attack on Israel that killed 1,200 people, according to Israeli tallies.
Israel's ensuing two-year campaign killed more than 72,000 Palestinians, according to Gazan health authorities, ŌĆŗand left the territory mostly in ruins.

item.title: ASEAN foreign ministers urge US and Iran to push for permanent resolution
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - ASEAN foreign ministers called on Monday for the United States ŌĆŗand Iran to continue negotiating a permanent ŌĆīend to their conflict, and to implement the ceasefire fully and effectively.
The ministers, who met virtually to discuss the war ŌĆŗin the Middle East, also called for the restoration of ŌĆŗsafe, unimpeded and continuous passage of vessels and ŌüĀaircraft in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Kremlin says it hopes for pragmatic ties with new Hungarian leadership after Orban defeat
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Monday it hoped to continue its "highly pragmatic ties" with ŌĆŗHungary's new political leadership after Prime Minister ŌĆīViktor Orban, a close Russian partner, was beaten at the ballot box by upstart rival Peter Magyar.
Orban, Hungary's veteran nationalist leader, lost power to Magyar ŌĆŗand his centre-right Tisza party in an ŌĆŗelection on Sunday after 16 years in office - ŌüĀa setback for his allies in Russia and ŌĆŗU.S. President Donald Trump's White House.
"Hungary has made its ŌĆŗchoice, and we respect that choice," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters.
"We look forward to continuing our highly pragmatic engagement ŌĆŗwith HungaryŌĆÖs new leadership. We noted MagyarŌĆÖs statement ŌĆŗregarding his willingness to engage in dialogue. Naturally, this will be ŌĆībeneficial ŌüĀfor both Moscow and Budapest."
Orban, who last visited Moscow in November, blocked a 90-billion euro ($105 billion) loan to Ukraine after accusing it of sabotaging Russian oil ŌĆŗdeliveries to ŌĆŗhis country, ŌüĀsomething Kyiv denied, and delayed and sometimes opposed EU sanctions on Russia.
Hungary has ŌĆŗalso continued to buy oil and gas ŌĆŗfrom ŌüĀRussia despite Moscow's war in Ukraine - which Orban said he did not want his country to be dragged ŌüĀinto - ŌĆŗand Moscow is building a new ŌĆŗnuclear power plant for Hungary.
($1 = 0.8559 euros)

item.title: Ghana says France is open to engaging on slavery reparations
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Ghana said France was open to having discussions with a coalition of countries that are calling for reparations for transatlantic slavery, following a meeting ŌĆŗlast week with President Emmanuel Macron.
Ghana's President John Dramani Mahama, accompanied ŌĆīby foreign minister Samuel Okudzeto Ablakwa and other officials, held talks with Macron in Paris on Wednesday last week.
Ablakwa said on X after the meeting that Macron has indicated ŌĆŗFrance was open to discussions on reparations, including the return of ŌĆŗlooted artefacts, addressing global economic inequities and dismantling structural racism.
An official ŌüĀfrom ├ēlys├®e Palace said on Sunday that both countries discussed France's efforts ŌĆŗto return culturally significant objects and human remains, as well as the legal ŌĆŗframeworks around these restitutions.
The official did not mention the additional measures cited by Ablakwa.
The meeting followed the United Nations' adoption last month of a Ghana-led resolution recognising slavery as the "gravest crime ŌĆŗagainst humanity" and calling for reparations. France, along with other European countries, abstained.
France's ŌĆŗrepresentative at the U.N. said the abstention was due to concerns that the resolution appeared ŌĆīto "establish ŌüĀa hierarchy among crimes against humanity".
Ablakwa said that despite that abstention, Macron had said France was willing to have an "open and honest dialogue" on the matter.
In 2001, France recognised transatlantic slavery as a crime against humanity. But, like most ŌĆŗEuropean nations, it ŌĆŗhas not formally ŌüĀapologised for its involvement or committed to reparations.
From the 15th to the 19th century, at least 12.5 million Africans were ŌĆŗkidnapped and forcibly transported by mostly European ships and ŌĆŗsold into ŌüĀslavery. France trafficked an estimated 1.3 million people, according to the Slave Voyages database.
Last year, Macron said he would set up a commission to examine France's past with ŌüĀHaiti.
Longstanding ŌĆŗcalls for reparations have gained momentum worldwide but so ŌĆŗhas a backlash , with critics arguing modern states should not be held accountable for historical wrongs.

item.title: Germany plans $1.9 billion of fuel price relief to tackle energy crisis
item.description:
BERLIN, April 13 (Reuters) - Germany's coalition government has agreed fuel price relief for consumers and businesses worth ŌĆī1.6 billion euros ($1.9 billion), ending a dispute over how to respond to an oil price surge triggered by the Iran war .
The energy tax on diesel and petrol will be cut by about 0.17 euros per litre for two months, the conservative CDU party and its ŌĆŗcentre-left SPD coalition partners said on Monday.
The Iran war has caused the biggest disruption to global energy supplies ŌĆŗon record, with plans for a U.S. blockade of Iranian ports and coastal areas further driving ŌüĀup crude prices .
"This war is the real cause of the problems we are experiencing in our own ŌĆŗcountry as well," Chancellor Friedrich Merz said at a press conference.
He said the coalition was doing everything possible to ŌĆŗcushion the impact of the conflict, which has been put on hold by a fragile ceasefire, and urged oil companies to pass the tax cut on in full. "We expect the oil industry to pass along these relief measures directly and fully to consumers," Merz ŌĆŗsaid.
Economists and industry groups were sceptical.
Marcel Fratzscher at economic research institute DIW Berlin said a large share of the ŌĆŗtax break could "end up in oil companies' bank accounts" and criticised the measures for failing to encourage fuel-saving.
Germany's filling station operators ŌĆīechoed that ŌüĀconcern, calling on the government to impose price controls on oil majors or risk them marking up prices to pocket part of the relief.
"The government needs to be tough on oil majors," a spokesperson told the Rheinische Post newspaper.
The coalition also agreed to let companies pay a 1,000 euro relief bonus per employee, exempt from payroll taxes ŌĆŗand social security contributions.
Weekend talks ŌĆŗappeared to defuse a dispute ŌüĀthat flared on Friday when Economy Minister Katherina Reiche, a Merz ally, criticised proposals from Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil of the SPD to impose a windfall tax on oil companies.
A ŌĆŗsource close to Merz told Reuters that Reiche's remarks undermined a push by the ŌĆŗchancellor to resolve ŌüĀcoalition disagreements quietly.
The government is under pressure to act as Europe's largest economy struggles with weak growth and disruptions from global trade tensions.
Merz said on Monday that Germany would oppose a planned 2027 tightening of European Union CO2 levies on ŌüĀhybrid ŌĆŗvehicles and would argue in Brussels for a more "technology-open" approach, including recognition ŌĆŗof cars running on renewable fuels.
The coalition is also preparing broader income tax cuts for lower- and middle-income groups from January 2027.
($1 = 0.8556 ŌĆŗeuros)

item.title: Japan risks summer power crunch due to Middle East LNG disruptions -IEEJ analyst
item.description:
TOKYO, April 13 (Reuters) - Japan may face a power supply crunch if the Middle East crisis drags on and liquefied natural gas shipments remain disrupted, just as ŌĆŗair-conditioning demand rises during the Japanese summer, an analyst at a leading ŌĆīenergy think tank said on Monday.
Takafumi Yanagisawa, executive analyst at the Institute of Energy Economics, Japan (IEEJ), said that a prolonged conflict could also delay the start of additional supplies expected by Japan ŌĆŗfrom 2028 from new projects in Qatar and the United Arab Emirates.

item.title: Nigeria seeks IMF, World Bank support as Iran shock hits reforms
item.description:
ABUJA, April 13 (Reuters) - Nigeria will seek stronger international financial support at this week's IMF-World Bank ŌĆīSpring Meetings as the Iran war lifts fuel costs at home and complicates reforms, its finance minister said on Monday.
Surging crude prices had some clear benefits for Africa's ŌĆŗtop oil producer, boosting foreign exchange earnings, Wale Edun said in ŌĆŗa statement ahead of this week's meetings in Washington.
"But the ŌüĀshock comes at a critical transition point, intensifying inflationary pressures and ŌĆŗraising living costs for households," he added.
Petrol prices have surged more than ŌĆŗ50% to 1,330 naira ($0.9788) per litre - and diesel more than 70% to 1,550 naira a litre - since the start of the conflict, hurting people and businesses, Edun said.
The jolting ŌĆŗchange threatened to derail reforms launched in 2023 to stabilise the economy ŌĆŗand revive growth.
President Bola Tinubu has rolled out Nigeria's most ambitious economic overhaul in ŌĆīdecades ŌüĀby ending costly fuel and energy subsidies, devaluing the currency and changing the tax system.
At this week's meetings, Edun, who chairs the G24 group of developing countries, said he planned to push for lower borrowing costs, ŌĆŗfairer global financial conditions ŌĆŗand more ŌüĀsupport for countries pursuing reforms.
Nigeria's benchmark Bonny Light crude grade climbed from about $70ŌĆō$73 a barrel before the conflict ŌĆŗto current levels above $120, the government said.
Inflation eased sharply ŌĆŗto 15.06% ŌüĀin February from around 33% in December 2024, but remains high compared with regional peers and has come under renewed pressure since the conflict began, ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗWorld Bank said last week.
Edun said the ŌĆŗgovernment would focus on attracting private investment, creating jobs and sustaining growth, while shielding vulnerable households ŌĆŗfrom rising prices.
($1 = 1,358.8200 naira)

item.title: Philippines' Marcos suspends taxes on fuel products
item.description:
MANILA, April 13 (Reuters) - Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr suspended taxes on kerosene and liquefied petroleum ŌĆŗgas on Monday to help consumers cope with ŌĆīrising fuel costs.
Congress had granted Marcos emergency powers to adjust fuel taxes, one of several measures the Philippines has ŌĆŗundertaken to cope with a sharp surge ŌĆŗin oil prices driven by the Middle East ŌüĀconflict.
Marcos said the temporary tax reduction cuts LPG prices by 3.36 ŌĆŗPhilippine pesos ($0.0559) per kilogram and almost 37 Philippine pesos ($0.6154) per tank ŌĆŗand 5.60 Philippine pesos ($0.0931) per liter, lowering costs for households that rely on it for lighting and cooking.
Marcos did not ŌĆŗsay how long the suspension will last, but ŌĆŗan interagency committee tasked with leading the government's response to ŌĆīthe ŌüĀenergy crisis will meet on April 14 to discuss whether to suspend, reduce, or maintain excise taxes on gasoline and diesel.
Diesel and gasoline prices in the ŌĆŗPhilippines have ŌĆŗmore than ŌüĀdoubled since the start of the Middle East conflict on February 28, as ŌĆŗthe country, which relies heavily on imported ŌĆŗfuel, ŌüĀfaces supply pressures amid a global price surge.
The Philippines' central bank warned last week of "spillover effects" from the Middle ŌüĀEast ŌĆŗconflict after headline inflation in ŌĆŗMarch hit 4.1% on an annual basis.
($1 = 60.1270 Philippine pesos)

item.title: EU should reconsider its plans to ban imports of Russian gas, Eni CEO says
item.description:
MILAN, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union should reconsider its plans to ŌĆŗprogressively ban imports of Russian ŌĆīgas from the start of 2027, Claudio Descalzi, the CEO of Italian ŌĆŗenergy group Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab said.
In ŌĆŗan address to an event organised ŌüĀby the co-ruling League ŌĆŗparty on Sunday, Descalzi was quoted ŌĆŗby Italian news agencies as saying it was unclear how the bloc could ŌĆŗreplace 20 billion cubic ŌĆŗmetres of gas from Russia, which provides ŌĆīvital ŌüĀflexibility to the output of power stations.
Russia is facing a European ban on Russian LNG imports ŌĆŗon short-term ŌĆŗcontracts ŌüĀfrom April 25 and on long-term contracts from ŌĆŗJanuary 1, 2027.
Descalzi said ŌĆŗthat ŌüĀin terms of the disruption of energy supplies, the conflict in ŌüĀIran ŌĆŗis "the most important ŌĆŗevent of the last 40 years."

item.title: India's HPCL issues rare tender seeking tanker to load Russian LPG, document shows
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - India's state-run Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab has issued a rare ŌĆŗtender seeking a liquefied petroleum gas tanker ŌĆīto immediately load propane and butane from Russia's Ust-Luga port, a tender document seen by Reuters ŌĆŗon Monday showed.
The vessel, to be loaded ŌĆŗwith 12,000 metric tons of butane and ŌüĀ8,000 tons of propane for discharge ŌĆŗon India's west coast, must not be under ŌĆŗsanctions and must have no links to Iran, the document said.
HPCL did not immediately respond to a ŌĆŗrequest for comment.
Indian state refiners are seeking ŌĆŗto buy LPG, used as cooking gas, from more diversified ŌĆīsources ŌüĀincluding Russia as India faces its worst LPG supply crisis in decades.
Supplies from the Middle East have been disrupted by the ŌĆŗclosure of the ŌĆŗStrait of ŌüĀHormuz following the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Indian authorities have previously said they ŌĆŗare purchasing LPG from countries including ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀU.S., Norway, Canada, and Russia.
India consumed 33.15 million tons of LPG last year, with imports ŌüĀaccounting ŌĆŗfor about 60% of ŌĆŗdemand. About 90% of those imports came from the Middle ŌĆŗEast.

item.title: CPC raises oil exports in March by 48% from February, sources say
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - The Caspian Pipeline Consortium (CPC), which supplies oil from Kazakhstan and Russia, increased crude exports by 48% in March, two industry ŌĆŗsources said on Monday, buoyed by a recovery ŌĆŗin output at the Tengiz oilfield.
Chevron-led (CVX.N) , opens new tab Tengiz is the ŌüĀlargest single field in Kazakhstan, a country that produces ŌĆŗ2% of the world's daily supply of crude oil, mainly ŌĆŗexported via CPC to the Russian port of Novorossiysk.
The field, operated by Tengizchevroil, went offline in January after power outages were reported ŌĆŗat the site near the Caspian Sea in western ŌĆŗKazakhstan.
Tengizchevroil's general director, William Lacobie, said last week that the field had returned ŌĆīto ŌüĀfull production.
The sources said that CPC, which pumps oil via the Tengiz-to-Novorossiysk pipeline, increased oil exports last month to 6.196 million metric tons, or 1.58 million barrels per day. ŌĆŗThat compared with ŌĆŗFebruary's 3.79 ŌüĀmillion tons, or 1.07 million bpd.
CPC declined to comment.
Challenges facing CPC have included Ukrainian ŌĆŗdrone attacks on the Russian terminal. It had ŌĆŗto ŌüĀhalt its operations for a few days after Ukraine attacked its facilities at the end of November, effectively destroying one of ŌüĀthree ŌĆŗfloating moorings.
Kazakhstan's energy ministry said on ŌĆŗTuesday that oil exports via CPC were stable after Russia's military had accused Ukraine ŌĆŗof damaging its loading facilities.

item.title: India's top solar state has renewable projects of about 60 GW awaiting transmission links
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - India's top solar energy-generating state of Rajasthan has clean energy projects of capacity amounting to ŌĆŗabout 60 gigawatt (GW) awaiting transmission links as planners struggle ŌĆīto keep pace with a rapid build-out, a regulatory filing showed.
The problem underscores a critical challenge for India in its effort to nearly double its ŌĆŗnon-fossil based power generation to 500 GW by 2030 ŌĆŗas the systems carry electricity to other states from ŌüĀrenewableŌĆærich regions such as Rajasthan.
Planner the Central Transmission Utility of India ŌĆŗLtd (CTUIL), told the national power regulator it had been unable to ŌĆŗprovide a transmission system for about 60 GW of projects.
The western desert state has 179 GW of renewable energy potential, with more than 85% of ŌĆŗprojects clustered in its four districts of Barmer, Bikaner, Jaisalmer ŌĆŗand Jodhpur, the April 10 filing showed.
Applications for grid connectivity totalling about 130 ŌĆīGW ŌüĀhave been received in Rajasthan, while transmission systems for only about 73 GW have been planned or are being set up, it added.
"CTUIL is facing challenges and difficulties in identification of corresponding transmission ŌĆŗsystem for 60 ŌĆŗGW applications," ŌüĀthe planner said.
The issue was highlighted after electricity regulators told Saurya Urja Company of Rajasthan Ltd, which ŌĆŗis developing a 400-megawatt solar park in Bikaner, ŌĆŗit ŌüĀcould withdraw its connectivity application and recover bank guarantees if needed.
The ruling by the Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC) came in response to challenges ŌüĀin ŌĆŗplanning transmission the company faced.
The regulator also ŌĆŗasked the transmission planner to advise project applicants about the transmission delays, allowing them ŌĆŗto withdraw connectivity applications.

item.title: Iran in touch with India on ships passage through Strait of Hormuz, Iranian envoy says
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - Iran has "good contact" ŌĆŗwith the Indian ŌĆīgovernment on passage of the South ŌĆŗAsian nation's ships ŌĆŗthrough the Strait of ŌüĀHormuz and ŌĆŗwants to help New ŌĆŗDelhi, Iranian Ambassador to India Mohammad Fathali ŌĆŗsaid on Monday.
The ŌĆŗU.S.-Israeli war on Iran, ŌĆīwhich ŌüĀbegan in February, has restricted shipping through the key ŌĆŗroute - ŌĆŗa ŌüĀconduit for 40% of India's ŌĆŗcrude imports - ŌĆŗimpacting ŌüĀtrade and squeezing oil supplies.

item.title: Russia calls for joint food reserves with BRICS to counter Middle East crisis risks
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 13 (Reuters) - Russia, the world's largest wheat exporter, should create joint food reserves with ŌĆŗfellow BRICS members and former Soviet neighbours to counter the risks to global food security ŌĆīstemming from the conflict in the Middle East , a senior Russian security official said on Monday.
About half of the world's food is grown using fertilizer, while one-third of global fertilizer trade used to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow ŌĆŗshipping lane along Iran's coast that has been largely closed since the conflict began.
"To ensure food ŌĆŗsecurity, it is highly important to expand cooperation with friendly countries, primarily the ŌüĀmember states of the Eurasian Economic Union and BRICS, including through the creation of joint food reserves," ŌĆŗAlexander Maslennikov, deputy secretary of Russia's Security Council, was quoted as saying by domestic news agencies.
Chaired by ŌĆŗPresident Vladimir Putin, the Security Council includes top officials and helps shape Kremlin decisions on major national security issues. Putin is due to meet BRICS member Indonesia's President Prabowo Subianto in the Kremlin on Monday with food security likely to feature ŌĆŗon the agenda.
Maslennikov said the Middle East crisis posed serious risks to global food security. If the ŌĆŗglobal fertilizer shortage persists until early summer, yields of major crops could fall by half, he said, fuelling the sharpest ŌĆīrise ŌüĀin world food inflation in recent years.
He added that the number of hungry people worldwide could rise to a record 673 million.
The World Bank, International Monetary Fund and the U.N. World Food Programme warned last week that sharp increases in oil, natural gas and fertilizer prices triggered by the war in the Middle East ŌĆŗwill inevitably cause rising ŌĆŗfood prices and food ŌüĀinsecurity.
Russia is a major producer and exporter of fertilizer but lacks the capacity to significantly increase output this year. It is also seeking to raise agricultural ŌĆŗexports by half by 2030.
Maslennikov said the current situation, while posing risks to ŌĆŗRussia's own ŌüĀfood security, also created long-term opportunities for the country's agricultural producers.
"Russia is in a strong position to increase food exports to the countries of the Middle East, as well as to Asia, Africa and Latin America," he ŌüĀsaid.
Egypt, ŌĆŗa BRICS member, is the largest importer of Russian wheat, ŌĆŗwhile Russia also exports food to China and India, the bloc's two biggest economies. The Eurasian Economic Union, led by Russia, also ŌĆŗincludes grain exporter Kazakhstan, as well as Belarus, Armenia and Kyrgyzstan.

item.title: Turkey says NATO should reset ties with Trump at next summit, prepare for future
item.description:
ANKARA, April 13 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Monday that NATO allies should use their July summit in Ankara to reset ties with U.S. ŌĆŗPresident Donald Trump and prepare for a potential reduction of ŌĆīU.S. involvement in the alliance.
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said that Turkey believed Trump would attend the NATO leaders' summit on July 7-8 due to his "personal ŌĆŗrespect" for President Tayyip Erdogan, but added he understood Trump ŌĆŗwas otherwise reluctant come to the meeting.
Trump has criticised NATO ŌüĀfor years and last week threatened to pull the United States ŌĆŗout of the alliance over European members' refusal to send ships to ŌĆŗunblock the Strait of Hormuz near Iran. That compounded friction within the bloc over his earlier plans to acquire Greenland.
Fidan told the state-owned Anadolu news ŌĆŗagency that allies had long considered Trump's criticisms to be ŌĆŗrhetoric, but were now planning around the possibility of reduced U.S. involvement and ramping ŌĆīup ŌüĀtheir own defensive capacities.
"NATO countries need to turn this Ankara Summit into an opportunity to put ties with the United States on a systematic basis," he said.
"If there will be a U.S. withdrawal ŌĆŗfrom some NATO ŌĆŗmechanisms, there needs ŌüĀto be a plan and programme to phase this out so nobody is left in the open," ŌĆŗhe added.
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte has said ŌĆŗhe understood ŌüĀTrump's frustrations with the alliance, but that the "large majority of European nations" had been helpful to Washington's war effort in Iran.
A senior White ŌüĀHouse ŌĆŗofficial told Reuters last week that Trump, ŌĆŗas part of frustration with NATO, had also considered the option of removing some U.S. ŌĆŗtroops from Europe.

item.title: EU member states must coordinate on energy prices amid Iran conflict, von der Leyen says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union's member states must coordinate on energy prices amid a ŌĆŗ22 billion euro ($25.70 billion) increase in fossil fuel ŌĆībills since the start of the Iran war, EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said on Monday.
"We're also looking into ... coordination ŌĆŗof member states' gas storage filling to avoid ŌĆŗthat many member states go to the market ŌüĀat the same time," von der Leyen told reporters ŌĆŗin Brussels.
"And we will coordinate oil stock releases, to achieve ŌĆŗthe largest possible effect, and we will ensure that member states' emergency measures will not impact the single market."
The EU Commission is planning ŌĆŗto publish proposals for energy price measures on April ŌĆŗ22, to be discussed by EU leaders at their informal summit ŌĆīnext ŌüĀweek.
Separately the EU's executive arm will present an electrification strategy before the summer, von der Leyen said as she stressed the need for structural measures to lower energy prices ŌĆŗas well.
"We ŌĆŗare paying ŌüĀa very high price for our global dependency on fossil fuels, and the grim reality ŌĆŗfor our continent is fossil fuel energy ŌĆŗwill ŌüĀremain the most expensive option in the years to come," von der Leyen said.
"Our strategy to decarbonize has not only ŌüĀbeen ŌĆŗconfirmed in the last years, but ŌĆŗis growing in importance day by day," she added.
($1 = 0.8559 euros)

BUDAPEST, April 13 (Reuters) - Hungary's opposition ŌĆŗleader Peter ŌĆīMagyar, whose party won ŌĆŗelections on ŌĆŗSunday with a ŌüĀlandslide, ŌĆŗsaid on Monday ŌĆŗthat his government would not ŌĆŗsupport ŌĆŗUkraine's fast-track entry to ŌĆīthe ŌüĀEuropean Union.
Magyar told a briefing ŌĆŗthat ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀissue of Ukraine's ŌĆŗmembership would ŌĆŗhave ŌüĀto go to ŌüĀa ŌĆŗreferendum.

item.title: NATO allies refuse to join Trump's Strait of Hormuz blockade
item.description:
LONDON/PARIS, April 13 (Reuters) - The United States' NATO allies said on Monday they would not get involved in President Donald Trump's plan to blockade ŌĆŗthe Strait of Hormuz , further ratcheting up tensions within the increasingly fragile alliance.
Trump said the U.S. ŌĆīmilitary would work with other countries to block all maritime traffic in the waterway, after weekend talks failed to reach an agreement to end the six-week conflict with Iran . The U.S. military later specified that the blockade , due to start at 1400 GMT on Monday, would only apply ŌĆŗto ships going to or from Iranian ports.
"The Blockade will begin shortly. Other Countries will be involved with ŌĆŗthis Blockade," Trump said in a post on Truth Social on Sunday.
But NATO allies including Britain ŌüĀand France said they would not be drawn into the conflict by taking part in the blockade, saying instead ŌĆŗthat it was vital to open the waterway through which one-fifth of the world's oil usually passes, which Iran has ŌĆŗeffectively closed since the conflict began on February 28.
Their refusal to participate is yet another point of friction with Trump, who has threatened to withdraw from the military alliance and is weighing pulling some U.S. troops from Europe after several countries resisted supporting the U.S. campaign against Iran ŌĆŗby denying U.S. military planes use of their airspace.
"We're not supporting the blockade," British Prime Minister Keir Starmer ŌĆŗtold the BBC.
"My decision has been very clearly that whatever the pressure, and there's been some considerable pressure, we're not getting dragged into ŌĆīthe ŌüĀwar," he said.
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte told European governments that Trump wants concrete commitments in the near future to help secure the Strait of Hormuz, diplomats told Reuters last week.
NATO could play a role in the strait if its 32 members could agree on the formation of a mission, Rutte said on April 9.
Several European countries have said they're willing to ŌĆŗhelp in the strait but ŌĆŗonly once there is a ŌüĀdurable end to hostilities and an agreement with Iran that their ships will not be attacked.
France will organise a conference with Britain and other countries to create a multinational mission ŌĆŗto restore navigation in the strait, French President Emmanuel Macron said on X on ŌĆŗMonday.
"This strictly defensive ŌüĀmission, distinct from the belligerents, will be deployed as soon as the situation allows," Macron said.
Britain is working on ways to reduce insurance premiums for ships passing through the strait once the fighting has stopped, according to a senior European official.
The Strait of ŌüĀHormuz ŌĆŗshould be reopened by diplomacy, Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said on ŌĆŗMonday, adding that creating an international force to oversee it would be complicated as he called for NATO to reset its ties with Trump at ŌĆŗa summit in Ankara in July.

item.title: Diplomatic push underway on Hormuz fertiliser proposal, UN says, as shortages bite
item.description:
GENEVA, April 13 (Reuters) - A diplomatic push is under way on a U.N.-led proposal to ensure safe passage for fertiliser ŌĆŗshipments through the Strait of Hormuz, the U.N. said on Monday, with ŌĆīa source familiar with discussions emphasising growing urgency due to shortages.
The six weeks of fighting sparked by U.S.-Israeli joint strikes on Iran, which spread to Lebanon and the Gulf, have ŌĆŗkilled thousands of people across the Middle East, upending global supply chains following ŌĆŗa near-total halt to shipping via the Strait of Hormuz.
The U.S. ŌüĀmilitary said it will begin a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and ŌĆŗexiting Iranian ports and coastal areas on Monday, after failed weekend talks to ŌĆŗend the war jeopardized a fragile two-week ceasefire.
There is an urgent need to find a solution, a source familiar with the U.N.-led initiative told Reuters, noting that some farmers ŌĆŗin Latin America are already skipping second corn plantings and that African ŌĆŗgrowers face a critical window, with severe yield losses looming without fertilisers.
"Extensive meetings" have been ŌĆīheld ŌüĀin New York over the past two weeks on the design and operationalisation of the mechanism with the relevant member states, Juliette Touma, UNOPS director of communications, told Reuters.
The meetings, led by Jorge Moreira da Silva, executive ŌĆŗdirector of the United ŌĆŗNations Office for ŌüĀProject Services, are ongoing, Touma added.
The proposed mechanism , opens new tab seeks to facilitate safe transit of commercial vessels carrying fertilizers and related ŌĆŗmaterials through the strait and mitigate potential humanitarian fallout on fertilizer-dependent ŌĆŗnations in ŌüĀAsia and Africa, she said.
A more-than-90% drop in tanker traffic is threatening agricultural production and global food security, the UN Food and Agriculture Organization says.
Businesses seek ŌüĀa ŌĆŗmechanism that would restore freedom of navigation without ŌĆŗthe need for naval escorts or tolls, the source familiar with discussions said, adding that the initiative could ŌĆŗeventually expand to other goods.

item.title: Pakistan PM Sharif says 'full effort' still on to resolve US-Iran conflict
item.description: ISLAMABAD, April 13 (Reuters) - Full efforts ŌĆŗare still ŌĆībeing made to ŌĆŗresolve the ŌĆŗconflict between the ŌüĀU.S. ŌĆŗand Iran, ŌĆŗPakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif ŌĆŗsaid ŌĆŗon Monday, a day ŌĆīafter ŌüĀtalks between the two ŌĆŗsides, ŌĆŗheld ŌüĀin Islamabad, ended ŌĆŗwithout ŌĆŗagreement.

item.title: Airlines cancel flights amid Middle East conflict
item.description:
April 13 - Global air travel remains severely disrupted, with many people unable to fly as planned to destinations after the Iran war forced the closure of major Middle Eastern hubs, including Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi.
Below is the latest on flights, in alphabetical order:
AEGEAN AIRLINES (AGNr.AT) , opens new tab
Greece's largest carrier cancelled flights to Riyadh and Amman until June 27 and to Tel Aviv and Beirut until June 26. It cancelled ŌĆŗflights to Erbil and Baghdad until July 2 and to Dubai until June 29.
Latvia's airBaltic says flights to Tel Aviv have been cancelled until May 31. Flights to Dubai ŌĆīare cancelled until October 24.
AIR CANADA (AC.TO) , opens new tab
The Canadian carrier has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv and Dubai until September 7.
The Spanish airline has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv until May 3.
AIR FRANCE-KLM (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab
Air France has suspended its Tel Aviv, Beirut, Dubai and Riyadh flights until May 3.
KLM has suspended flights to Riyadh, Dammam and Dubai until May 17.
CATHAY PACIFIC (0293.HK) , opens new tab
The Hong Kong airline has cancelled flights to Dubai and Riyadh until June 30. To cater for a surge in demand to Europe, it will operate ŌĆŗextra passenger flights to London, Paris and Zurich in April.
DELTA (DAL.N) , opens new tab
The U.S. carrier has cancelled its New York-Tel Aviv flights and delayed the restart of its Atlanta-Tel Aviv route until September 5. It ŌĆŗsaid the launch of its Boston-Tel Aviv route, planned for late October, has been delayed until further notice.
EL AL ISRAEL AIRLINES (ELAL.TA) , opens new tab
The Israeli carrier said customers who ŌüĀplanned to depart Israel through April 18 have had their flights cancelled, including relevant return flights.
It will increase the number of destinations to about 30 from April 13 and will gradually expand that number through ŌĆŗthe rest of the month.
The UAE airline has said it is operating a reduced flight schedule following a partial reopening of regional airspace.
The UAE carrier has said it is operating a commercial flight schedule between ŌĆŗAbu Dhabi and around 80 destinations.
FINNAIR (FIA1S.HE) , opens new tab
The Finnish carrier cancelled its Doha flights until July 2, while continuing to avoid the airspace of Iraq, Iran, Syria and Israel. The airline only restarts its Dubai flights in October.
FLYNAS (4264.SE) , opens new tab
The Saudi budget airline has suspended flights to Dubai, Abu Dhabi, Sharjah, Doha, Bahrain, Kuwait, Iraq and Syria until April 15.
IAG (ICAG.L) , opens new tab
IAG-owned British Airways is reducing flights to the Middle East when services resume, permanently dropping Jeddah as a destination, while adding capacity to India and Africa.
It ŌĆŗplans to reduce services to Dubai, Doha and Tel Aviv to one daily flight from JulyŌĆ» 1, and to cut Riyadh services from two daily flights to one from midŌĆæMay. Changes apply through the summer ŌĆŗseason that ends on October 24, with one Dubai service restarting on October 16.
IAG's Spanish low-cost airline Iberia Express has cancelled flights to Tel Aviv through May 31.
JAPAN AIRLINES (9201.T) , opens new tab
Japan Airlines has suspended scheduled Tokyo-Doha flights until May 10 and ŌĆīDoha-Tokyo flights ŌüĀuntil May 11. The airline also announced extra flights between Tokyo and London on April 25.
The Polish airline suspended its flights to Tel Aviv until May 31. It also cancelled flights to Riyadh until June 30 and to Beirut from March 31 to May 30. The airline plans to operate its winter route to Dubai in October.
LUFTHANSA GROUP (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab
Lufthansa, Swiss, Austrian Airlines, Brussels Airlines, ITA Airways and Edelweiss suspended flights to Dubai and Tel Aviv until May 31, and to Abu Dhabi, Amman, Beirut, Dammam, Riyadh, Erbil, Muscat and Tehran until October 24. Lufthansa Cargo is the same, except for the Tel Aviv suspension, which will last through April ŌĆŗ30.
Low-cost carrier Eurowings plans to suspend flights to Tel ŌĆŗAviv, Beirut and Erbil through April 30 and ŌüĀto Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Amman through October 24.
The Malaysian carrier suspended flights to Doha until June 14.
NORWEGIAN AIR (NAS.OL) , opens new tab
The low-cost airline has pushed back planned launches of its Tel Aviv and Beirut services to June 15.
PEGASUS (PGSUS.IS) , opens new tab
Turkey's Pegasus Airlines cancelled its Iran, Iraq, Amman, Beirut, Kuwait, Bahrain, Doha, Dammam, Riyadh, Dubai, Abu Dhabi ŌĆŗand Sharjah flights until May 1.
The Moroccan carrier says flights to Doha are cancelled until June 30 and those to Dubai until May ŌĆŗ31.
QANTAS (QAN.AX) , opens new tab
Australia's flag carrier is adding ŌüĀflights to Rome and Paris to meet an upswing in demand for European routes. Flights to Paris will increase to five return flights per week from three and the Perth-Singapore service will increase from daily to 10 flights per week. An updated schedule will come into effect progressively for flights from mid-April and run until late July.
The carrier said it is gradually increasing flights from Doha to more than 120 destinations by mid-May.
SINGAPORE AIRLINES (SIAL.SI) , opens new tab
The carrier ŌüĀextended its ŌĆŗSingapore-Dubai flights suspension until May 31, while adding services on the Singapore-London Gatwick and Singapore-Melbourne routes from late March until October ŌĆŗ24 to meet higher demand.
TURKISH AIRLINES (THYAO.IS) , opens new tab
SunExpress, Turkish Airlines' joint venture with Lufthansa, has cancelled flights to Dubai until April 30.
WIZZ AIR (WIZZ.L) , opens new tab
The low-cost airline suspended flights to Israel until April 25, and to Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Amman from mainland European destinations until mid-September. All ŌĆŗflights to Medina have been suspended indefinitely.

item.title: Intergovernmental deal for $25 billion Nigeria-Morocco gas pipeline due this year, official
item.description:
RABAT, April 13 (Reuters) - An intergovernmental agreement (IGA) on a planned $25 billion Nigeria-Morocco gas pipeline will be signed this year, the head of Morocco's hydrocarbons and mining ŌĆŗagency (ONHYM) said.
Agreed a decade ago, the project - known as the African ŌĆīAtlantic Gas Pipeline - would run 6,900 km on a hybrid offshore-onshore route with a maximum capacity of 30 billion cubic metres (bcm), including 15 bcm to supply Morocco and support exports to ŌĆŗEurope, ONHYM's Amina Benkhadra told Reuters by email.
The pipeline, which has the ŌĆŗbacking of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS), has completed ŌüĀits feasibility study and front-end engineering design (FEED) stages.
Following the intergovernmental agreement, a high ŌĆŗauthority for the pipeline will be established in Nigeria, bringing together ministerial representatives from ŌĆŗeach of the 13 participating countries to provide political and regulatory coordination, Benkhadra said.
A project company will also be created in Morocco as a joint venture between ONHYM and the Nigerian ŌĆŗNational Petroleum Company (NNPC) to lead the execution, financing and construction phase, she said.
The ŌĆŗpipeline would spur economic integration across West Africa by expanding electricity generation and facilitating industrial and ŌĆīmining ŌüĀdevelopment, while helping Morocco position itself as an energy bridge between Africa and Europe, she said.
Initial segments of the project would connect Morocco to gas fields in Mauritania and Senegal, and link Ghana to Cote d'Ivoire further south, before a final ŌĆŗsegment connects Ghana to ŌĆŗNigeria's gas fields, ŌüĀshe said.
First gas from the initial phases is expected in 2031, Benkhadra said.
"The project does not rely on a single ŌĆŗglobal final investment decision," she said, adding that each segment ŌĆŗis designed ŌüĀto be developed as "standalone system" to allow for early value build up, she said.
No final funding commitments have been secured yet, she said, adding that the financing structure ŌüĀwill be ŌĆŗled by the project company, which will mobilise ŌĆŗa mix of equity and debt.
"The project is attracting strong interest due to its scale, its phased ŌĆŗstructure, and its strategic positioning," Benkhadra said.

item.title: India gets first Iranian oil in 7 years, ship tracking data shows
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - Two very large crude carriers loaded with Iranian oil have reached ŌĆŗIndian ports, ship tracking data from LSEG ŌĆīshows, as local refiners utilise a temporary waiver granted by the United States last month to resume purchases from ŌĆŗTehran for the first time in seven ŌĆŗyears.
The current waiver is due to expire on ŌüĀApril 19.
The Iran-flagged Felicity has reached Sikka ŌĆŗPort in western India, while the Curacao-flagged Jaya ŌĆŗis at the eastern port of Odisha, the data shows.
A VLCC carries 2 million barrels of oil.
India, the world's third-biggest ŌĆŗoil importer and consumer, has not received ŌĆŗa cargo from Iran since May 2019 after coming under ŌĆīU.S. ŌüĀpressure not to buy the country's crude.
Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab , the country's top refiner, has bought Iranian oil loaded on the Jaya, a vessel under U.S. ŌĆŗsanctions, Reuters ŌĆŗreported last ŌüĀweek.
India has also allowed Reliance Industries Ltd (RELI.NS) , opens new tab , the operator of the world's ŌĆŗbiggest refining complex, to buy Iranian oil ŌĆŗloaded ŌüĀon the Comoros-flagged aframax Kaviz, Curacao-flagged VLCC Lenore and Iran-flagged VLCCs Felicity and Hedy, all of which are ŌüĀmore ŌĆŗthan 20 years old and ŌĆŗare also under U.S. sanctions.

item.title: UBS raises Brent oil price view as Strait┬Āof Hormuz disruptions persist
item.description:
April 13 - The path of least resistance for crude oil prices continues to remain upward as long as flows through the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz stay restricted, UBS said in a note on Monday ŌĆīas it raised its Brent crude prices forecasts.
The bank increased its Brent price forecast to $100 a barrel by the end of June 2026, $95 by the end of September, and $90 ŌĆŗby the end of December. It also expects Brent to trade at $85 ŌĆŗa barrel by the end of March 2027.
"There is elevated uncertainty ŌüĀin respect to these forecasts depending on how the conflict evolves, what ŌĆŗenergy infrastructure is disrupted, and when flows through the strait resume," it noted.
A ŌĆŗprolonged disruption would raise the risk of a short-term price overshoot, with Brent potentially trading above $150 a barrel, while heightening the likelihood of demand destruction, UBS said.
It added that scarcity-driven ŌĆŗhoarding could further amplify price swings.
